title: Video by code_with_gary
caption:
Day 34/365 If you treat RAG like static data, you’re one update away from a production outage. 

The Simple Breakdown

• Layer 1: Source Data. Every document needs a version ID or timestamp. Never overwrite your files—use snapshots so you can always see what the model knew at a specific point in time.

• Layer 2: Embeddings. If you change your embedding model or how you “chunk” text, your old vectors become useless. You have to version the model and the logic together so you can roll back if the AI starts hallucinating.

• Layer 3: The Index. Never update an index while it’s live. Build a new index in the background, test it, and then “swap” the traffic over. This ensures zero downtime.

• Traceability: Every piece of data should carry metadata like the ingestion date and source. This makes it easy to filter for only the latest info or debug why a specific answer was generated.

• The Re-indexing Choice: You don’t always need a full rebuild. Use incremental updates for small changes and save the full re-indexing for major model or logic shifts.

• Testing: Before pushing a new version live, check the accuracy and latency. Only “promote” it to production once it passes those quality gates.

The Interview One-Liner
“RAG versioning is handled through Alias-based switching and Metadata tagging. By building new indexes in parallel and using version filters, you ensure the system stays live and the data stays accurate.”
